_target_label: FALSE — threat vs action (Trump threat ≠ ordered destruction)
_domain_hint: Iran / Israel / Houthi / IRGC theatre. Sources: Reuters, AP, Al Jazeera, BBC, IDF spokesperson, IRGC statements, Iranian state media (IRNA/Tasnim/Mehr/Press TV), UKMTO maritime bulletins, ISW Iran updates.
claim: President Trump ordered the destruction of Iran's Kharg Island oil infrastructure in April 2026.
claim_origin: Misreading of Trump threat statement as action announcement

sources.text: If for any reason a deal is not shortly reached, which it probably will be, and if the Hormuz Strait is not immediately 'Open for Business,' we will conclude our lovely 'stay' in Iran by blowing up and completely obliterating all of their Electric Generating Plants, Oil Wells and Kharg Island (and possibly all desalinization plants!), which we have purposefully not yet 'touched.'
I have chosen NOT to wipe out the Oil Infrastructure on the Island.
Kharg Island's oil terminal remains undamaged. US officials have explicitly avoided hitting energy infrastructure to limit global oil disruptions.

gold.rationale_summary: Trump's April 15 statement is a conditional future threat ('if a deal is not shortly reached... we will conclude... by blowing up'), not an order or announcement of executed action. The statement explicitly says 'we have purposefully not yet touched' the oil infrastructure — confirming non-action through April 15. Trump's earlier March 14 statement explicitly preserved oil infrastructure. Polymarket context confirms oil infrastructure remained undamaged through April 30. The claim conflates a conditional threat with an executed order — these are categorically different in evidence-evaluation.
required_caveats: Trump did THREATEN to destroy Kharg oil infrastructure as future retaliation — but threats are not orders.
Trump's own language ('not yet touched', 'will conclude') confirms non-action through statement date.
Pattern: belligerent leaders frequently issue conditional threats as negotiation leverage; conflating threats with actions is a known reasoning error.
